question_text: Which source backbone is used for U10 - Death and Dying?
rationale: U10 is built from Module 16 - Death and Dying, which covers grief, end-of-life needs, signs of approaching death, comfort measures, hospice, and postmortem boundaries.

question_text: Scenario: A hospice resident has long pauses in breathing and a dry mouth. A family member asks if death will happen tonight. What is the safest CNA response?
answer_a: Tell the family death will probably happen tonight.
answer_b: Acknowledge the concern, report the breathing and dry mouth to the nurse, provide assigned comfort care, and refer prognosis questions to the nurse or hospice contact.
answer_c: Say the family should stop asking because it upsets staff.
answer_d: Enter the real resident's details into the online course for advice.
rationale: The CNA supports privacy and comfort, reports observed changes, and refers prognosis questions; predicting time of death or using PHI exceeds the role and course guardrails.

question_text: Scenario: A dying resident says, 'I am scared to be alone tonight.' Which action is outside CNA scope?
answer_a: Stay briefly if duties allow and speak calmly.
answer_b: Report the fear to the nurse so the care team can respond.
answer_c: Promise that you will arrange medication or sedation so the resident sleeps.
answer_d: Offer privacy and comfort measures that are in the care plan.
rationale: CNAs may offer presence, comfort, reporting, and privacy, but medication decisions and promises about treatment are outside CNA scope.

question_text: Which note is the most objective for a resident near end of life?
answer_a: Resident is being dramatic and family is making it worse.
answer_c: Family is wrong about what they saw.
answer_d: Resident slept most of evening; breathing had pauses of about 20 seconds; mouth dry; daughter asked about condition; nurse notified.
rationale: Objective reporting describes observable facts, resident or family statements, timing, and notification without diagnosis, blame, or prognosis.

question_text: In this unit, what does hospice mean?
answer_a: A certificate approval number for the course
answer_b: Care focused on comfort, quality of life, and support for a person with terminal illness and family
answer_d: A rule that all routine care stops
rationale: Hospice emphasizes comfort and quality of life while the CNA continues assigned personal care, observation, dignity protection, and reporting.

question_text: Scenario: A family asks for quiet time for a cultural or spiritual practice at the bedside. What should the CNA do first?
answer_a: Remove personal items so the room looks standard.
answer_b: Tell the family that spiritual practices are not part of care.
answer_c: Protect privacy as allowed, respect the request within policy, and notify the nurse of any needs or questions.
answer_d: Ask the family to describe the ritual in the Moodle assignment using real names.
rationale: End-of-life care includes respect for emotional, spiritual, and cultural needs while maintaining privacy, policy boundaries, and no-PHI course rules.

question_text: Which cluster of observations near end of life should the CNA report promptly to the nurse?
answer_a: A family member quietly holds the resident's hand.
answer_b: The room lights are dimmed at the resident's preference.
answer_c: New restlessness, mottled feet, much less urine, and breathing with long pauses.
answer_d: A visitor reads softly from a book.
rationale: Restlessness, mottling, decreased urine output, and changed breathing can occur as death approaches and should be reported as observations within CNA scope.

question_text: Scenario: After a resident appears to have died, what is the safest CNA action?
answer_a: Remove jewelry and medical devices before anyone else enters.
answer_b: Tell nearby visitors the details so they understand what happened.
answer_c: Begin postmortem care immediately without direction.
answer_d: Protect privacy, notify or follow the nurse's direction, and wait for authorized confirmation and policy-guided assignments.
rationale: The CNA does not pronounce death or independently handle belongings/devices; postmortem care follows facility policy, legal requirements, and licensed direction.

question_text: Which right continues for a dying resident?
answer_a: The right to dignity, privacy, respectful care, comfort support, and choices when possible
answer_b: The right to have staff predict the exact time of death
answer_c: The right to have private details shared in class for realism
answer_d: The right to have all cultural preferences ignored for staff convenience
rationale: Dying residents retain rights to dignity, privacy, respectful care, comfort, and appropriate choice; prognosis predictions and privacy violations are unsafe.

question_text: Which comfort measure is generally within the CNA role when assigned in the care plan?
answer_a: Changing the oxygen setting because breathing sounds different
answer_b: Providing mouth care, repositioning with pillows, smoothing linens, reducing noise, and reporting pain cues
answer_c: Interpreting a DNR order for the family
answer_d: Deciding that the resident no longer needs observation
rationale: Assigned mouth care, positioning support, clean linens, calm environment, and reporting pain cues are CNA comfort measures; changing orders or interpreting legal documents is outside scope.
